From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Liturgical period, immediately following the Christmas season

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Pr%C3%A4st_och_ministranter_i_Mariakyrkan_i_Sigtuna.jpg/330px-Pr%C3%A4st_och_ministranter_i_Mariakyrkan_i_Sigtuna.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pr%C3%A4st_och_ministranter_i_Mariakyrkan_i_Sigtuna.jpg)

The priest and altar servers of [Saint Mary's Evangelical-Lutheran Church](/wiki/St._Mary%27s_Church,_Sigtuna "St. Mary's Church, Sigtuna") standing in the [narthex](/wiki/Narthex "Narthex") of during [recessional hymn](/wiki/Processional_hymn#Recessional_hymn "Processional hymn") at the end of Epiphany Day [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Edward_Burne-Jones_-_The_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/500px-Edward_Burne-Jones_-_The_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edward_Burne-Jones_-_The_Adoration_of_the_Magi_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[The Adoration of the Magi](/wiki/The_Adoration_of_the_Magi "The Adoration of the Magi")* by [Edward Burne-Jones](/wiki/Edward_Burne-Jones "Edward Burne-Jones") (1904)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Epiphany_Season_door_chalking.jpg/250px-Epiphany_Season_door_chalking.jpg)](/wiki/File:Epiphany_Season_door_chalking.jpg)

The Epiphanytide tradition of [chalking the door](/wiki/Chalking_the_door "Chalking the door") involves writing C✝M✝B (representing the names of the [Three Wise Men](/wiki/Three_Wise_Men "Three Wise Men") as well as the [Christian prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") *Christus mansionem benedicat*) with the year flanking both sides on one's door, as seen here on an apartment door in the [Midwestern US](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States").

| [Liturgical seasons](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") |
| --- |
| * Pre-Christmas   + [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") (*Western*)   + [Nativity Fast](/wiki/Nativity_Fast "Nativity Fast") (*Byzantine*)   + [Annunciation](/wiki/Season_of_Annunciation "Season of Annunciation") (*Syriac*) * [Christmas](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide") * Epiphany   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent") * [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (*Western*) / [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") (*Eastern*) * [Paschal Triduum](/wiki/Paschal_Triduum "Paschal Triduum") (*Western*) * [Easter](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") * [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost_season "Pentecost season")   + [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time") (*Western*) * [Summer](/wiki/Season_of_Summer "Season of Summer") (*East Syriac*) * [Apostles' Fast](/wiki/Apostles%27_Fast "Apostles' Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Dormition Fast](/wiki/Dormition_Fast "Dormition Fast") (*Eastern*) * [Elijah–Cross–Moses](/w/index.php?title=Elijah%E2%80%93Cross%E2%80%93Moses&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elijah–Cross–Moses (page does not exist)") (*East Syriac*) * [Dedication of the Church](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_the_Dedication_of_the_Church&action=edit&redlink=1 "Season of the Dedication of the Church (page does not exist)") (*Syriac*) |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Liturgical_year "Template:Liturgical year") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Liturgical_year "Template talk:Liturgical year") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical_year "Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical year") |

The **Epiphany season**, also known as **Epiphanytide** or the time of **Sundays after Epiphany**, is a [liturgical period](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy"), celebrated by many [Christian Churches](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination"), which immediately follows the [Christmas season](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide"). It begins on [Epiphany Day](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"), and ends at various points (such as [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas")) as defined by those denominations. The typical [liturgical color](/wiki/Liturgical_colours "Liturgical colours") for the day of Epiphany is white, and the typical color for Epiphany season is green.

Popular Epiphanytide customs include [Epiphany singing](/wiki/Star_singers "Star singers"), [chalking the door](/wiki/Chalking_the_door "Chalking the door") and families inviting their pastor to [bless their home](/wiki/House_blessing#Christianity "House blessing").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Western Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Western Christianity")]

### Roman Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Roman Rite")]

#### Ordinary Form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ordinary Form")]

The [Ordinary Form](/wiki/Ordinary_Form "Ordinary Form") of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") regards the time after Epiphany and before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") as part of [Ordinary Time](/wiki/Ordinary_Time "Ordinary Time").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Some Catholics[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] believe the Christmas Season ends on the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord"), which is typically celebrated on the Sunday after Epiphany. Although Epiphany is not a distinct season, the Ordinary Form does have specific [Mass](/wiki/Mass_in_the_Catholic_Church "Mass in the Catholic Church") [collects](/wiki/Collect "Collect") that are used from Epiphany onward.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Because Epiphany is, in many places, transferred to the Sunday between January 2 and 8 inclusive, the period during which the Epiphany texts are used forms a *de facto* [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)") between the Sunday of Epiphany and the Sunday of the Baptism of the Lord.

#### Extraordinary Form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Extraordinary Form")]

In [traditionalist Catholic](/wiki/Traditionalist_Catholicism "Traditionalist Catholicism") communities that use the [General Roman Calendar of 1960](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_1960 "General Roman Calendar of 1960") as part of the [Extraordinary Form](/wiki/Extraordinary_Form "Extraordinary Form") authorized by *[Summorum Pontificum](/wiki/Summorum_Pontificum "Summorum Pontificum")*, Epiphany is celebrated with a *de facto* [octave](/wiki/Octave_(liturgy) "Octave (liturgy)") from January 6 to the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord") on January 13, although the octave was nominally removed in the [calendar reforms of 1955](/wiki/General_Roman_Calendar_of_Pope_Pius_XII "General Roman Calendar of Pope Pius XII"). The Sundays which follow are designated "Sundays after Epiphany" or "Time after Epiphany" until the start of [Pre-Lent](/wiki/Pre-Lent "Pre-Lent").[[4]](#cite_note-Lefebvre1999-4)

#### Ordinariate Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ordinariate Use")]

In 2015, the Catholic Church authorized a Use of the Roman Rite for the three [Personal Ordinariates](/wiki/Personal_ordinariate "Personal ordinariate") for former Anglicans. The [Ordinariate Use](/wiki/Ordinariate_Use "Ordinariate Use") explicitly includes a period called Epiphanytide, which runs from the Monday after the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord") until the day before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Moravianism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Moravianism")]

In the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), the Epiphany season runs until the Sunday before Ash Wednesday.[[6]](#cite_note-Moravian2012-6) While white is the liturgical colour used for the Feast of the Epiphany itself, the Sundays during the rest of the season use green.[[6]](#cite_note-Moravian2012-6)

### Lutheranism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Lutheranism")]

In [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), Epiphanytide runs from [Epiphany Day](/wiki/Epiphany_Day "Epiphany Day") until [Septuagesima Sunday](/wiki/Septuagesima_Sunday "Septuagesima Sunday")—three Sundays before [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday")—(as with the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in Denmark](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_Denmark "Evangelical Lutheran Church in Denmark")),[[7]](#cite_note-7) or from Epiphany Day until the day before [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday") (as with the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America")).[[8]](#cite_note-ELCA2011-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Anglicanism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Anglicanism")]

There are provided six "Sundays after Epiphany" in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*, to be used for the varying number of Sundays after Epiphany until [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2000, the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") introduced into its liturgy an optional Epiphany season by approving the *[Common Worship](/wiki/Common_Worship "Common Worship")* series of services as an alternative to those in the *[Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer")*. This optional season begins with [Evening Prayer](/wiki/Evening_Prayer_(Anglican) "Evening Prayer (Anglican)") on the day before the Epiphany (which may be celebrated on January 6 or on the Sunday between January 2 and 8) and ends on [Candlemas](/wiki/Candlemas "Candlemas"), which celebrates the [Presentation of Jesus at the Temple](/wiki/Presentation_of_Jesus_at_the_Temple "Presentation of Jesus at the Temple").[[11]](#cite_note-11) (which may be celebrated on February 2 or on the Sunday between January 28 and February 3).

### Methodism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Methodism")]

In [Methodism](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism"), the Epiphany season runs from [Epiphany Day](/wiki/Epiphany_Day "Epiphany Day") until [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), the first day of Lent.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) White is liturgical colour for Epiphany Day itself, as well as for the [Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Baptism_of_the_Lord "Baptism of the Lord") and the [Feast of the Transfiguration](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Transfiguration "Feast of the Transfiguration"), while green is the liturgical colour used for the rest of the season.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Eastern Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eastern Christianity")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [[icon]](/wiki/File:Wiki_letter_w_cropped.svg) | This section **needs expansion** with: subsections about other Eastern rites. You can help by [adding missing information](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=8). *(December 2020)* |

### Greek Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Greek Rite")]

In the [Greek Rite](/wiki/Greek_Rite "Greek Rite") (used by various [Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") and [Byzantine Catholic](/wiki/Byzantine_Catholic "Byzantine Catholic") churches), the Feast of the Epiphany takes place on January 6. The [Liturgy of St. Basil](/wiki/Liturgy_of_St._Basil "Liturgy of St. Basil") is celebrated with Vespers on the eve of the feast; and the Vigil is made up of Great Compline and Matins. The [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy") tells the story of Jesus's baptism. After Vespers and the Divine Liturgy, the Great Blessing of the Water takes place. The celebrant immerses the cross into the water three times and then blesses the people with the water.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It is customary in theses churches for the faithful to drink the water and to take it home for use throughout the year.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### East Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: East Syriac Rite")]

In the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") (used by churches such as the [Syro-Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church")), this period is called the Season of Epiphany, also known by its Syriac [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") *Denha*. This season begins on the Sunday between January 2 and 6, or on January 6 itself if no such Sunday exists. The season runs until the first Sunday of Lent, which begins seven weeks before Easter (three days earlier than it does in Western Christianity).

The rite celebrates the following feast days on sequential Fridays during Epiphany season:[[17]](#cite_note-17)

1. St. [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist")
2. Sts. [Peter and Paul](/wiki/Feast_of_Saints_Peter_and_Paul "Feast of Saints Peter and Paul")
3. The [Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists")
4. St. [Stephen](/wiki/Saint_Stephen "Saint Stephen")
5. The Greek Doctors ([Diodorus](/wiki/Diodorus_of_Tarsus "Diodorus of Tarsus"), [Theodorus](/wiki/Theodore_of_Mopsuestia "Theodore of Mopsuestia") and [Nestorius](/wiki/Nestorius "Nestorius"))
6. The Syriac Doctors ([Aprem](/wiki/Ephrem_the_Syrian "Ephrem the Syrian"), [Narsai](/wiki/Narsai "Narsai") and [Abraham](/wiki/Abraham_the_Great_of_Kashkar "Abraham the Great of Kashkar"))
7. The Patron of the Church
8. [Commemoration of the Dead](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day")

Because the length of the Season of Epiphany is variable, later Friday feasts are sometimes moved to earlier weeks.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The [Three Days' Lent](/wiki/Fast_of_Nineveh "Fast of Nineveh") occurs during this season.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### West Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: West Syriac Rite")]

In the [West Syriac Rite](/wiki/West_Syriac_Rite "West Syriac Rite") (used by the [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church"), the [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church"), the [Syriac Catholic Church](/wiki/Syriac_Catholic_Church "Syriac Catholic Church") and various Malankara Churches of India), the season begins on January 6 on the [Feast of the Baptism of the Lord](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Baptism_of_the_Lord "Feast of the Baptism of the Lord"), called Denho ("appearance" or "sunrise") in [West Syriac dialect](/wiki/West_Syriac_dialect "West Syriac dialect").[[20]](#cite_note-20) For some Syriac churches, this season may be the traditional time of reception of [catechumens](/wiki/Catechesis "Catechesis") into the Church. In Maronite culture many people wait to have their babies initiated (i.e., baptized and chrismated) on or after Denho.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Epiphany_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: References")]

* [![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/P_christianity.svg/40px-P_christianity.svg.png)](/wiki/File:P_christianity.svg)[Christianity portal](/wiki/Portal:Christianity "Portal:Christianity")
* [![icon](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/046CupolaSPietro.jpg/40px-046CupolaSPietro.jpg)](/wiki/File:046CupolaSPietro.jpg)[Catholicism portal](/wiki/Portal:Catholicism "Portal:Catholicism")

"The Christmas season is often celebrated for twelve days, ending with the Epiphany.
